models.agent: An AI persona with a name, personality instructions, assigned TTS voice, and creativity settings. Users create multiple agents but only one is active during commentary.
models.voice: A TTS voice option available from the configured TTS provider. Each agent is assigned a voice.
models.commentary: A single AI-generated comment tied to a timestamp in the video. Displayed as subtitle overlay and in the chat sidebar.
models.videoSource: The video being watched, either a URL or an uploaded file reference.

relationships: An agent has one active voice assigned from the available voices list
A commentary item is generated by the active agent and tied to a video timestamp
Only one agent can be selected as the active commentator at a time

agents.name: RiffMaster
agents.personality: Slapstick comedian who makes pop culture references and groan-worthy puns. Loves pointing out plot holes with exaggerated reactions.

agents.name: Professor Snark
agents.personality: Dry, academic wit. Delivers commentary like a bored film critic who's seen it all. Uses big words incorrectly just to be annoying.

agents.name: Cosmos Companion
agents.personality: Educational commentator who explains the real science behind sci-fi movies. Points out what's accurate and what's pure Hollywood magic.

agents.name: The Duo
agents.personality: Simulates two personalities: a slapstick comic and a dry wit who banter back and forth. 'Hey, did you see that? That explosion defied at least three laws of physics!' 'Yes, Dave, very impressive. Can we focus on the plot? Oh wait, there isn't one.'

commentary.text: Oh great, another movie that starts with someone waking up. Revolutionary! Next you'll tell me the protagonist has a vague sense of unfulfillment.
That explosion was so big it probably registered on the Richter scale AND defied the laws of thermodynamics. Science: 0, Hollywood: 1.
Ah yes, the classic 'walking slowly toward the camera while dramatic music plays' shot. Cinema at its most subtle.
The star field shown here has approximately 40 visible stars per square degree. The actual Milky Way has 1,000-4,000. Still, it's a beautiful artistic choice representing our cosmic neighborhood.
Did you see that car flip? Three times! It's like the director watched too many action movies and said 'hold my beer.' 'To be fair, the stunt driver probably earned every penny for that one.'
Wait, they're running in slow motion now. Is it for drama? Is it for effect? Or did the director just really like that song and didn't want it to end?
